title: AI Risk Governance for Security Programs

modules.title: Module 1: Why Traditional Compliance Frameworks Fail AI Systems
modules.sub: Exposing the gaps legacy compliance leaves in AI environments
modules.description: Learners examine the structural limitations of frameworks like SOC 2 and ISO 27001 when applied to AI-driven systems. They identify specific compliance blind spots — such as model drift and opaque decision logic — that create undetected risk. By the end, learners can articulate why AI demands a distinct governance approach.

modules.title: Module 2: Mapping AI-Specific Risks — Model Poisoning, Data Leakage, and Shadow AI
modules.sub: Identifying and categorizing the unique threat surface of AI systems
modules.description: Learners work through a structured risk taxonomy covering model poisoning, training data exfiltration, and unsanctioned AI deployments. They apply a threat-modeling lens to map these risks against their organization's AI assets and data flows. The output is a prioritized AI-specific risk register ready for governance review.

modules.title: Module 3: Applying NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001, and Emerging Regulatory Standards
modules.sub: Translating regulatory frameworks into actionable AI governance requirements
modules.description: Learners compare the NIST AI Risk Management Framework and ISO 42001 alongside emerging mandates such as the EU AI Act to understand their compliance obligations. They map framework controls to real AI use cases within a security program context. By the end, learners can select and justify the appropriate framework mix for their organization's risk profile.

modules.title: Module 4: Building an AI Security Policy — Controls, Accountability, and Documentation
modules.sub: Drafting enforceable policies that assign ownership and define AI controls
modules.description: Learners draft the core components of an AI security policy, including acceptable use boundaries, control specifications, and accountability assignments. They work through a policy template to align technical controls with governance requirements and stakeholder roles. The deliverable is a structured policy document defensible under regulatory scrutiny.

modules.title: Module 5: Audit Readiness — Evidence Collection and AI System Attestation
modules.sub: Preparing documentation and evidence packages that satisfy auditor expectations
modules.description: Learners identify what auditors look for when reviewing AI systems, including model cards, data lineage records, and control test results. They practice assembling an evidence package that demonstrates control effectiveness across the AI lifecycle. By the end, learners can produce and organize audit artifacts that support AI system attestation.

modules.title: Module 6: Incident Response and Breach Disclosure in AI Contexts
modules.sub: Adapting incident response plans for AI-specific failures and disclosure obligations
modules.description: Learners analyze how AI incidents — such as model compromise or biased output causing harm — differ from conventional security breaches in both scope and disclosure requirements. They update a sample incident response playbook to incorporate AI-specific detection, containment, and notification steps. The result is a response plan aligned with regulatory breach disclosure timelines and AI governance expectations.

modules.title: Module 7: Continuous Monitoring and Governance Maturity Roadmaps
modules.sub: Sustaining AI governance through monitoring cadences and maturity progression
modules.description: Learners design a continuous monitoring plan that tracks model performance, control effectiveness, and emerging AI risks over time. They assess their organization's current governance maturity using a structured rubric and identify the next-stage improvements to pursue. The final output is a prioritized governance maturity roadmap with defined milestones and ownership.

synopsis: When AI systems are deployed without governance structures, organizations face regulatory penalties, undetected model compromises, and liability exposure that traditional compliance audits are blind to. This course equips risk and compliance professionals with a working framework to assess, document, and defend AI security programs against real regulatory scrutiny.
rationale: Regulators including the EU AI Act, SEC, and NIST are now demanding demonstrable AI risk governance, but most security compliance programs were built before AI systems existed at scale.
learning_outcome: Learners can conduct an AI risk assessment, map controls to current regulatory frameworks, and produce audit-ready documentation for an AI-integrated security program.
source_signals: NIST Releases New Control Overlays to Manage Cybersecurity Risks in AI Systems
Trump AI plan calls for cybersecurity assessments, threat info-sharing
Microsoft Releases 2025 Digital Defense Report: Highlighting the Changing Cyber Threat Landscape and the Importance of Security in the AI Era
Cybersecurity 2025: Rising AI threats, new AI tools
